entry.title: Re: Email verification for provisioned users - IAS
entry.content:
Hello  @Yogananda

thank you very much for your support!

I still have an issue with the email verification behavior in IAS.

Some users already existed in IAS (they were provisioned earlier). After that, I changed my mapping in the source system (IdP) like this:

{
    "user": {
        "mappings": [
            {
                "sourcePath": "$",
                "targetPath": "$"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.id",
                "targetVariable": "entityIdSourceSystem"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.userName",
                "correlationAttribute": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.emails[?(@.primary== true)].value",
                "optional": true,
                "correlationAttribute": true
            },
            {
                "targetPath": "$.id",
                "type": "remove"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.employeeID",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User']['employeeID']"
            },
            {
                "targetPath": "$.meta",
                "type": "remove"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.enabled",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.active"
            },
            {
                "constant": false,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['sendMail']",
                "scope": "createEntity"
            },
            {
                "constant": true,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['mailVerified']",
                "scope": "createEntity"
            }
        ]
    },
    "group": {
        "ignore": false,
        "mappings": [
            {
                "sourcePath": "$",
                "targetPath": "$"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.id",
                "targetVariable": "entityIdSourceSystem"
            },
            {
                "targetPath": "$.id",
                "type": "remove"
            },
            {
                "targetPath": "$.meta",
                "type": "remove"
            }
        ]
    }
}

Then I ran a resync job, but the existing users in IAS were not set to "Verified".

After that, I deleted one user in IAS and provisioned it again.
→ This user was created correctly and the email was verified.

However, after running another resync job, the verification was removed again (the user is "Not Verified" again).

My question:
How can I make sure that the email stays verified for already provisioned users and is not overwritten during resync?

Thank you very much

Best Regards

}

entry.title: Re: Overhead is Zero on Event-Based Costing
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Sorry for the follow-up.

I think I may have identified the cause. In the costing sheet, I did not maintain all the required G/L accounts for the credit side of each overhead row.

For example, I defined 5 overhead rows but only maintained G/L accounts for 2 of them. I expected at least those 2 to be posted, but no overhead was posted at all.

After maintaining G/L accounts for all overhead rows, the overhead started to be calculated and posted correctly.

Could you please confirm if this behavior is correct, i.e., the system requires all overhead rows in the costing sheet to have corresponding G/L accounts maintained, otherwise no overhead will be posted?

Thank you!

entry.title: The SAP BW Migration Decision: Your Real Options in 2026
entry.content:
The question most of the Clients with BW system are asking is What do we actually do about SAP BW?

This isn't a theoretical question anymore. BW 7.5 mainstream maintenance ends in 2027. Private Cloud Edition (PCE) extends that runway to 2030—but that's barely four years away, and four years moves fast when you're carrying years of accumulated business logic, custom transformations, and deeply embedded reporting dependencies. The pressure to act is real, and the cost of making the wrong call—getting trapped in a costly rework cycle or betting on a platform without a future—is significant.

What follows are the options being actively debated on the ground in 2026. Not vendor positioning. Not marketing decks. The actual trade-offs architects and platform owners are wrestling with.

Setting the Stakes: Why This Decision Is Harder Than It Looks

On the surface, "migrate off BW" sounds like a straightforward modernization exercise. In practice, it's one of the most consequential architectural decisions an SAP organization can make right now. The choice determines:

  • How much of your existing system logic can be used as-is versus needs to be rebuilt from scratch
  • How deeply your organization wants to leverage AI and cloud-native capabilities in future
  • Your Organization’s dependency on the SAP ecosystem versus willingness move beyond to operate across platforms
  • The total cost of system change over the next 5–10 years, not just the migration cost

Option Comparison at a Glance

Option

Maintenance horizon

BW dependency

Migration effort

Innovation ceiling

AI/ML readiness

Best for

1 · BW 7.5 PCE
Lift to private cloud

2030

Full

★☆☆☆☆

Low

★☆☆☆☆

Time/budget pressure, deferred decision

2 · BW/4HANA PCE
Convert + extend runway

2040

Partial

★★★☆☆

Medium

★★☆☆☆

Existing BW/4HANA investment, incremental approach

3 · BDC Greenfield
Custom data products + accelerators

2040+

Zero

★★★★★

High

★★★★★

Transformation mandate, S/4 or ECC, AI priority

4 · BDC SAP Data Products
SAP-managed content layer

2040+

Zero

★★★★☆

High

★★★★☆

RISE with S/4HANA, SAP-native preference

5 · Non-SAP Lakehouse
Snowflake / Fabric / BigQuery etc.

Platform-defined

Zero

★★★★★

Highest

★★★★☆

Strong eng. team, multi-cloud, platform independence

Migration effort and AI/ML readiness scored 1–5 stars. AI/ML readiness reflects native platform capability, not bolt-on integrations.

Option 1 · SAP BW 7.5 PCE — The Stability Bridge

Overview

Lift your existing NetWeaver BW 7.5 environment into SAP's managed Private Cloud Edition, hosted in the Business Data Cloud (BDC). Models, logic, and processes are preserved. You get auto-patching and managed infrastructure without a significant architecture change.

Architecture

Option 1 · SAP BW 7.5 PCE  |  Maintenance to 2030

Sources feed unchanged BW 7.5 inside a SAP-managed private cloud boundary. Consumption via existing BO/SAC stack.

S/4HANA / ECC / Other sources → SAP BW 7.5 (unchanged) → Auto-patch · managed ops → SAC / BO reporting

Limitation: No new capabilities. Bridge to 2030, not a destination. Same decision awaits in 4 years.

 

The appeal

This is the lowest-disruption path. If your organization has deep BW investments—complex transformations, heavily customized InfoProviders, years of tuning—this option protects all of it while buying time. Maintenance is covered through 2030, and the operational burden shifts to SAP.

The reality check

You're essentially paying to extend a legacy architecture's life. There's no meaningful innovation on this path—no cloud-native scaling, no real AI/ML depth, no pathway to modern data product paradigms. When 2030 arrives, you'll face the same decision you're facing today, except with less time and potentially more technical debt. This is a bridge, not a destination.

Best suited for: Organizations under significant time or budget pressure that need to stabilize the platform and defer a larger transformation decision—while using the time window deliberately to plan a real migration.

Option 2 · SAP BW/4HANA PCE — The Extended Runway

Overview

Convert or lift to BW/4HANA in Private Cloud Edition, gaining a supported path through 2040. You get HANA-optimized performance, simplified modelling, and initial exposure to BDC capabilities via the Data Product Generator.

Architecture

Option 2 · SAP BW/4HANA PCE  |  Maintenance to 2040

Convert BW objects to BW/4HANA in managed PCE. Simplified modeling and initial BDC exposure via Data Product Generator.

S/4HANA / ECC / Other sources → SAP BW/4HANA (HANA-native) → Data Product Generator (limited) → SAC / BO reporting

Limitation: Warehouse-centric ceiling remains. "Better old" rather than cloud-native new. Innovation gap widens over time.

 

The appeal

This feels like a real modernization move. The modelling paradigm is cleaner, HANA performance is materially better, and you're buying 15+ years of supported life. For organizations that have invested heavily in BW and want to preserve that investment while incrementally modernizing, this path feels responsible.

The reality check

The honest framing here is "better old" rather than truly new. BW/4HANA is still a warehouse-centric architecture at its core. You're not going to be building intelligent, composable data products or running embedded AI agents off a BW/4HANA foundation the way you can on a native BDC or lakehouse architecture. The 2040 maintenance date is reassuring—but the innovation gap may become uncomfortable well before then.

Best suited for: SAP-committed organizations with significant BW/4HANA conversion work already underway, or those for whom a greenfield re-platform is genuinely not feasible in the near term.

Option 3 · Greenfield BDC — Custom Data Products + Accelerators

Overview

A complete architectural reset. Zero BW. Sources (S/4HANA, ECC, and others) are extracted directly, modelled in SAP Datasphere, and delivered as custom and derived data products. AI agents and implementation accelerators automate significant portions of model builds, semantic layer enrichment, and data product generation.

Architecture

Option 3 · BDC Greenfield — Custom Data Products + Accelerators

Zero BW. Direct source extraction into Datasphere, modeled as custom/derived data products. AI agents and partner accelerators automate semantic enrichment and model builds.

S/4HANA · ECC · 3rd party → SAP Datasphere (semantic modeling) → Custom / derived data products → AI Data Studio agents → SAC · Intelligent apps · APIs

Accelerator advantage: Partner-built tooling compresses model build timelines significantly. Pre-built content reduces blank-page risk.

Investment: Highest upfront redesign effort. Business logic re-engineering required. Strong change management needed.

 

The appeal

This is the path generating the most excitement in architect conversations right now, and for good reason. It breaks the BW dependency entirely, aligns to the direction SAP is actually investing in, and opens the door to genuinely modern capabilities: composable data products, real AI integration, intelligent applications, and cloud-native scalability. Well-designed accelerator tooling has demonstrated material productivity gains—in some cases compressing what would be months of manual build work into weeks.

The reality check

This is also the highest-investment path upfront. Greenfield means greenfield—existing models don't migrate, business logic must be re-examined and re-implemented, and the organisational change management burden is real. The question isn't whether this path is technically superior (for most organizations, it is). The question is whether your organization has the capacity, sponsorship, and appetite for the transformation effort required.

Best suited for: Organizations ready to commit to a true platform shift, particularly those with S/4HANA or active ECC landscapes and a clear mandate to modernize analytics.

Option 4 · Greenfield BDC — SAP-Managed Data Products

Overview

Similar to Option 3, but leveraging SAP's own curated and managed data products as the foundation layer rather than fully custom-built data products. Integration with platforms like Databricks is supported. No BW dependency.

Architecture

Option 4 · BDC Greenfield — SAP-Managed Data Products

Zero BW. SAP-curated data products as foundation layer. Databricks integration for advanced analytics. Best suited for RISE with S/4HANA customers.

S/4HANA (RISE) + ECC (limited) → SAP Datasphere → SAP-managed data products (curated) → Databricks integration → SAC · Intelligent apps · APIs

Advantage: Lower blank-page risk. SAP-curated foundation reduces build effort for core business domains.

Constraint: Optimised for RISE with S/4HANA. ECC and hybrid landscapes face integration friction.

 

The appeal

SAP-managed data products represent a significant investment by SAP in pre-built, semantically enriched content covering core business domains. For organizations running RISE with S/4HANA, this path can significantly reduce the "blank page" problem—you're building on a curated foundation rather than starting from scratch.

The reality check

SAP's managed data products are currently best optimized for RISE with S/4HANA customers. Organizations running ECC, hybrid landscapes, or non-SAP source systems will encounter integration friction, access challenges, or additional build work that narrows the advantage.

Best suited for: RISE with S/4HANA customers looking for an SAP-native, lower-custom-development path to BDC adoption.

Option 5 · Re-platform to a Non-SAP Lakehouse or Data Platform

Overview

SAP source data—extracted via CDS views or other mechanisms, often with BDC handling the extraction and sharing layer—lands in a non-SAP platform of choice: Snowflake, Databricks, Google BigQuery, Microsoft Fabric, or Amazon Redshift. BW is eliminated entirely. Analytics and AI/ML are built natively on the target platform.

Architecture

SAP data extracted via CDS views and BDC APIs, landed directly into a non-SAP target platform. BW eliminated entirely. Analytics and AI/ML built natively on the target platform.

Extraction layer:
S/4HANA · ECC · SAP apps → CDS views / BDC Data Sharing APIs / ETL

Target platforms (choose one):
Snowflake Databricks Microsoft Fabric Google BigQuery Amazon Redshift

Consumption:
BI / analytics layer Native ML / AI models Data apps / APIs Non-SAP data unification

Important nuance: Even on this path, BDC typically handles clean SAP data extraction and sharing. Full SAP bypass is more complex than it appears.

Key advantages: Maximum flexibility · cost control · best-of-breed AI/ML · no SAP ecosystem lock-in · non-SAP data unification.

Key constraints: Highest rebuild effort · business logic re-engineering required · strong data engineering team essential.

 

The appeal

Maximum flexibility. You're no longer constrained by SAP's licensing model, release cadence, or ecosystem boundaries. AI and ML capabilities on platforms like Databricks and BigQuery are mature, deep, and actively evolving. Cost control is real. And for organizations with significant non-SAP data to unify with SAP data, this path eliminates integration awkwardness.

The reality check

This path demands the most migration work and the deepest rebuild. Business logic encoded in BW transformations doesn't port automatically—it requires deliberate re-engineering. Even on this path, BDC typically plays a role in clean extraction and sharing of SAP data, so the idea of completely bypassing the SAP data layer is more complex than it initially appears.

Best suited for: Organizations with strong data engineering capabilities, significant non-SAP data integration requirements, or a strategic commitment to platform independence and best-of-breed architecture.

The Bottom Line: Where the Momentum Is Building

Across conversations on the ground in 2026, a clear pattern is emerging. The PCE options are being chosen by organizations that need to buy time, not by organizations with genuine transformation ambition. They're legitimate decisions for the right circumstances, but few architecture leaders describe them as where they want to end up.

The real energy is in the greenfield re-platform paths—either to native BDC or to a non-SAP lakehouse. The driving force is AI. Organizations that can see what's possible when analytics and intelligence are natively integrated are struggling to get excited about warehouse-centric architectures, regardless of how long the maintenance window extends.

The practical hurdles are consistent across every conversation:

  • Rework effort — The sheer volume of business logic, calculation rules, and custom transformations encoded in mature BW environments is sobering.
  • Preserving deep business logic — Much of the logic in long-running BW systems isn't well-documented. Migration is as much a knowledge recovery exercise as a technical one.
  • Licensing and timeline — The commercial complexity of BDC licensing, RISE entitlements, and overlap with existing BW investments is a real architectural constraint.
  • Skills availability — Datasphere, BDC, and native lakehouse architectures require skill sets that are in high demand and not evenly distributed.

The Question Worth Sitting With

The migration off SAP BW isn't primarily a technology question—it's a strategic one. The technology options are well-defined. What varies by organization is the honest assessment of transformation capacity, the clarity of the analytics strategy, and the willingness to invest now to avoid a harder reckoning later.

Where is your organization leaning? SAP-native BDC greenfield, accelerated custom build, or full non-SAP platform independence? And what's your biggest real constraint—rework effort, preserving business logic, licensing complexity, or the skills gap?

The architects who navigate this best in the next 24 months won't be the ones who picked the objectively "right" platform. They'll be the ones who were clearest about what their organization could actually execute—and built a path that matched that reality.

entry.title: Re: Error in Outbound Delivery Replication from S4/HANA to SAP LGM
entry.content: @seymaisleyen - what is the originating action in ERP for this outbound delivery? i.e is it an outbound delivery for a sales order, or were you trying to process something like a stock transfer out of yout LGM location?
